name: Torsion Field Energy
description: Torsion field energy, also referred to as spin field or axion field, is a pseudoscientific concept originating primarily in Russia in the late 20th century. Proponents claim that torsion fields are a fundamental force of nature beyond the four known fundamental forces (gravity, electromagnetism, strong nuclear force, and weak nuclear force). They assert that these fields are generated by spinning objects or geometric shapes and can propagate through space at speeds far exceeding the speed of light, influencing physical systems and even consciousness. The theory gained traction within some scientific circles in Russia, particularly in the context of military and technological applications, but has been widely discredited by the international scientific community. Claims include the ability to transmit information instantaneously across vast distances, to heal illnesses, and to alter the physical properties of matter. Evidence cited often involves unconventional experiments with spinning objects, geometric shapes, and alleged energy emissions. Mainstream science refutes these claims, citing methodological flaws in the experiments, lack of reproducible results, and the absence of a theoretical framework consistent with established physics. Torsion field theory is considered a fringe science lacking empirical validation, and its purported applications are viewed with skepticism.
subtitle: Pseudoscience of spinning fields and energy.

claims.claim: Torsion fields can transmit information faster than light.
claims.evidence: Experiments involving spinning objects and detectors showing instantaneous responses.
claims.criticism: No reproducible evidence exists. Observed effects are attributed to experimental error, confounding variables, or confirmation bias. Violates established laws of physics.

claims.claim: Torsion fields can heal diseases and improve health.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports and uncontrolled studies claiming positive health outcomes from exposure to torsion field generators.
claims.criticism: Lack of rigorous clinical trials and placebo controls. No scientifically plausible mechanism of action. Attributed to placebo effect or misinterpretation of data.

claims.claim: Torsion fields can alter the physical properties of matter.
claims.evidence: Experiments showing changes in the structure or behavior of materials exposed to torsion fields.
claims.criticism: Poorly designed experiments with inadequate controls. Results are not reproducible by independent researchers. No credible explanation for the claimed effects.

keyFigures.name: Gennady Shipov
keyFigures.role: One of the main proponents and developers of torsion field theory.

keyFigures.name: Anatoly Akimov
keyFigures.role: A key figure in the development and promotion of torsion field research in Russia.

keyFigures.name: Evgeny Alexandrov
keyFigures.role: A prominent Russian physicist who has strongly criticized torsion field theory.

mythologyConnections: Possible interpretations of subtle energies in folklore
Connections to shamanic practices involving altered states of consciousness

timeline.event: Development of torsion field theory in Russia by Shipov, Akimov, and others.
Peak of research and development efforts in torsion field technologies, particularly in military contexts.
Continued promotion of torsion field theory by some researchers and its widespread rejection by the mainstream scientific community.

sources.author: A.E. Akimov
sources.work: Heuristic Discussion of the Problem of Finding Long-Range Interactions
sources.title: A.E. Akimov
